rationale: The citing paper uses Dhar and Simonson (2003) in core parts of Study 4 to justify how preference fluency is manipulated. It explicitly cites that paper for the claim that presenting attribute values as ranges (vs. points) makes preference/attitude formation more difficult, and it follows that logic in its own design and interpretation. Because this citation supports a key experimental manipulation and boundary-condition argument, it plays a meaningful functional role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Dhar and Simonson's ambiguity manipulation logic (range vs. point attribute values) to a new question: whether private self-awareness effects on compromise choice depend on preference fluency. It imports that prior idea to operationalize choice difficulty and interpret the attenuation of self-awareness effects.
